rubric.rationale: The diff covers most requested surfaces: ACP flags, adapter mget methods, validation email fallback, delete cleanup, and explicit expires timestamps. However, there are correctness risks. loadUserInfo treats confirmation objects with missing/non-numeric expires as neither pending nor expired, which may leave legacy or malformed records unclear despite the requested expired/missing status. canSendValidation is suspicious: it computes ttl as remaining expiry time, then compares ttl + interval < max, which likely preserves the old TTL-based logic but may not correctly use the stored absolute expires timestamp as requested. sendValidationEmail no longer applies database TTL, but confirm:byUid and confirm:<code> are only cleaned on explicit checks, so stale records may accumulate. The ACP does not attach an explicit missing flag. The database mget implementations mostly preserve order/nulls, but Mongo returns either data or value and may not exactly match the string storage contract. Scope is mostly disciplined, with little unrelated churn, but the expiry/resend logic feels partially adapted rather than fully rethought around the new explicit expiry model.

rubric.rationale: The diff implements most requested surfaces: mget across adapters, confirmation objects with expires timestamps, pending/expired ACP flags, validation email fallback, resend email override, and deletion cleanup. However confidence is only moderate. The ACP template still gates pending/expired display behind the presence of a profile email, so users whose email only exists in the confirmation object may still show the wrong status. No explicit missing status is modeled. Mongo mget does not filter to string records/type, so as a generic db.get-equivalent it may return the wrong document when keys collide across object types. Existing valid TTL-based confirmation records without an expires field will now fail confirmation immediately, which is a migration/backcompat risk. The canSendValidation logic mostly preserves the old TTL math but still partly relies on db.pttl and has edge cases around missing expires. Overall this is a competent partial fix, but it leaves meaningful correctness and UI-status gaps.

+def fetch_google_book(isbn: str) -> dict[str, Any] | None:
+    """
+    Fetch Google Books volume metadata for a single ISBN.
+    """
+    try:
+        response = requests.get(f"{GOOGLE_BOOKS_API_URL}?q=isbn:{isbn}")
+    except requests.RequestException:
+        logger.exception("Google Books fetch failed for ISBN %s", isbn)
+        return None
+
+    if response.status_code != 200:
+        logger.warning(
+            "Google Books fetch failed for ISBN %s with HTTP %s",
+            isbn,
+            response.status_code,
+        )
+        return None
+
+    try:
+        return response.json()
+    except ValueError:
+        logger.warning("Google Books returned invalid JSON for ISBN %s", isbn)
+        return None
+
+
+def process_google_book(google_book_data: dict) -> dict[str, Any] | None:
+    """
+    Convert a Google Books API response into an Open Library import record.
+    """
+    items = google_book_data.get("items") or []
+    total_items = google_book_data.get("totalItems", len(items))
+
+    if total_items == 0 or not items:
+        return None
+
+    if total_items != 1 or len(items) != 1:
+        logger.warning(
+            "Google Books returned %s results for a single ISBN query; skipping",
+            total_items or len(items),
+        )
+        return None
+
+    item = items[0]
+    volume_info = item.get("volumeInfo") or {}
+    identifiers = volume_info.get("industryIdentifiers") or []
+    isbn_10 = [
+        identifier["identifier"]
+        for identifier in identifiers
+        if identifier.get("type") == "ISBN_10" and identifier.get("identifier")
+    ]
+    isbn_13 = [
+        identifier["identifier"]
+        for identifier in identifiers
+        if identifier.get("type") == "ISBN_13" and identifier.get("identifier")
+    ]
+
+    title = volume_info.get("title")
+    if not title or not isbn_13:
+        return None
+
+    book = {
+        "isbn_10": isbn_10,
+        "isbn_13": isbn_13,
+        "title": title,
+        "subtitle": volume_info.get("subtitle") or "",
+        "authors": [
+            {"name": author} for author in volume_info.get("authors", []) if author
+        ],
+        "source_records": [f"google_books:{isbn_13[0]}"],
+        "publishers": (
+            [volume_info["publisher"]] if volume_info.get("publisher") else []
+        ),
+        "publish_date": volume_info.get("publishedDate") or "",
+        "number_of_pages": volume_info.get("pageCount"),
+        "description": volume_info.get("description") or "",
+    }
+    return {key: value for key, value in book.items() if value is not None}
+
+
+def _stage_google_book_metadata(isbn: str) -> dict[str, Any] | None:
+    google_book = fetch_google_book(isbn)
+    if not google_book:
+        return None
+
+    book = process_google_book(google_book)

+

rubric.rationale: The diff addresses several required touchpoints, but correctness is shaky. The biggest concern is staging Google Books items with ia_id/source_records as `google_books:<isbn13>` while the accepted staged source added is `google_books`; Open Library source_records commonly use `source:id`, so this likely will not be recognized as intended. The fallback also only handles ISBN-13-only requests or Amazon not-found paths, but the broader import enrichment path may not reliably find the staged item by ISBN. The worker refactor is unrelated to the feature and creates collateral risk in a long-running threaded service, including changed exception/stat behavior and no queue task_done handling. Parsing is partially competent, including zero/multiple result handling and missing authors, but it is bare-bones, has no timeout, and may reject valid Google records too aggressively. The promise import change matches the requested BookWorm staging URL, assuming imports already include requests, but error handling is minimal. Overall this is a partial implementation with real feature intent, but likely integration bugs and unnecessary churn.

rubric.rationale: The diff addresses several named requirements superficially, but likely does not work end to end. The biggest correctness risk is staging/retrieval mismatch: Google data is added to batch `google` with `ia_id` set to `google_books:<isbn>`, then looked up via `ImportItem.find_staged_or_pending(identifiers=[isbn_13], sources=["google_books"])`; depending on the existing import indexing, this may not be discoverable. The batch/source name also does not clearly match the new staged source. The Google parser rejects responses with `totalItems != 1`, which can skip valid single returned `items` when Google reports a larger total. It also emits `authors` as `{name: ...}` objects, which may not match the import field shape expected by the pipeline. The affiliate handler now accepts ISBN-13-only requests as `key` even though Amazon lookup paths were originally keyed by ISBN-10 or B*ASIN, risking behavior changes before fallback. The new worker classes are speculative churn unrelated to the feature. Root cause is only partially addressed: a Google Books path exists, but persistence and lookup integration look too fragile for reliable enrichment.
